channel.title: NYT > Climate and Environment
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Suspect in Hacking of Climate Activists Is Extradited to New York
item.description: Prosecutors say Amit Forlit ran a global hacking operation on behalf of a Washington lobbying group that aimed to thwart environmental lawsuits against oil companies.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Brandon Bell/Getty Images
item.media:description: The clients of the lobbying firm that is alleged to have hired Amit Forlit included Exxon Mobil, according to a legal filing.

item.title: Forest Service Will Close Research Stations That Study Wildfire Risk
item.description: Scientists say their work on fires and climate change could be lost as the agency moves its headquarters to Utah from Washington and shuts 57 research stations.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:credit: John Stember for The New York Times

item.title: Republicans Seek Protections for Oil Giants Against Climate Lawsuits
item.description: Utah passed a law to insulate oil companies against efforts to make them pay for their role in global warming. A nationwide version is in the works.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles and Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Micah Green for The New York Times
item.media:description: Exxon, Chevron and ConocoPhillips face potentially costly legal claims over the role of fossil fuels in global warming. A Chevron facility in Mississippi.

item.title: E.P.A. Targets Microplastics and Drugs in Drinking Water
item.description: The move drew praise from leaders of the Make America Healthy Again movement who had recently criticized the agency’s handling of toxic chemicals.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi and Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Ben Mckeown/Associated Press
item.media:description: The move could unlock more federal research into how drinking water can be treated to remove pollutants, but it may also lead to costly new standards for water utilities.

item.title: Clean Energy Team Wins Salt River Project Election in Arizona
item.description: Proponents of renewable power will control the Phoenix area utility’s policymaking for the first time after they won an unusually contentious race that drew attention from national groups.
item.dc:creator: Reis Thebault
item.media:credit: Vanessa Abbitt/The Republic, via Imagn Images
item.media:description: The Salt River Project Agua Fria Generating Station in Glendale, Ariz., powers much of the Phoenix valley.

item.title: How War in the Middle East Paralyzed an Asian Food Giant
item.description: Vietnam, the world’s No. 2 rice exporter, cut production as power prices surged. Even with a temporary cease-fire in Iran, worries linger over the world’s food supply.
item.dc:creator: Damien Cave and Linh Pham
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A boat transporting newly harvested rice in My Thanh Ward, Dong Thap Province, Vietnam, in late March.

item.title: Heat Wipes Out Western Snowpack, Raising Fears of Summer Drought
item.description: A record-mild winter and blistering spring heat will mean higher risks of water shortages and wildfires in the months ahead.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance, Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Mira Rojanasakul
item.media:credit: Cheney Orr/Reuters
item.media:description: A puddle of water at the Keystone Ski Resort as temperatures reach into the mid 60s in Keystone, Colo., on March 21.

item.title: Why Electric Trucks Haven’t Taken Off in the U.S.
item.description: The vast majority of commercial trucks in the U.S. run on diesel, but that may be changing as fuel prices rise and Tesla ramps up production.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: An electric truck in the Port of Los Angeles in 2023.

item.title: How Tolls in the Strait of Hormuz Would Undercut International Law
item.description: Both the U.S. and Iran are now talking about imposing fees on ships using the vital passageway. That could fray a treaty that ended centuries of lawlessness on the high seas.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Associated Press
item.media:description: A cargo ship in the Persian Gulf heading toward the Strait of Hormuz in recent weeks.

item.title: Hockey Rinks Turn to Plastic Ice as Planet Warms
item.description: Some environmentalists question using plastic to address rising temperatures.
item.dc:creator: Ken Belson and Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:credit: Rebecca Gratz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Climate change has wreaked havoc with many sports. But few sports have been affected as much as those played on ice and snow.

item.title: Hershey’s Promises to Use Only Real Chocolate After Backlash
item.description: The change follows weeks of complaints from a grandson of the Reese’s Peanut Butter Cup inventor and other vocal consumers.
item.dc:creator: Alice Callahan
item.media:credit: Mike Blake/Reuters
item.media:description: The Hershey Company said it would return to using “classic milk and dark chocolate recipes” in all of its products by 2027, adding that it was responding to consumer preferences.

item.title: Trump Said Gas Prices Would Fall Quickly. World Leaders Are Not So Sure.
item.description: The president said this week the recent increases would be “short-term.” But other leaders are preparing for a longer crisis.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump addressing the nation from the White House on Wednesday.

item.title: Trump’s Budget May Target Climate Programs. Here’s What Survived the Last Cuts.
item.description: Efforts to study, prepare for and address climate change have taken a major hit. But a few programs are hanging on thanks to bipartisan support.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Congress has prevented the dismantling of popular programs like Energy Star and the Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program.

item.title: This Puppy Could Someday Save Your Life
item.description: Avalanche rescue dogs are essential tools for ski patrollers searching for people buried in the snow, and these special companions start their training young.
item.dc:creator: Christine Chung and Amber Baesler
item.media:description: Baggs “likes people, but she also really likes cruising around and sniffing,” said her owner, Rob Brennan, a Jackson Hole ski patroller.

item.title: In New England, Catching Climate Data Along With Fish
item.description: Commercial vessels are deploying high-tech sensors to map a shifting sea, providing critical data for scientists and some help for the industry.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler and Tristan Spinski
item.media:credit: Tristan Spinski for The New York Times

item.title: The Three Big Questions Delaying New York’s Budget
item.description: Legislators are weighing tax increases on the wealthy and changes to laws meant to protect immigrants and the environment as the state budget deadline passes.
item.dc:creator: Grace Ashford
item.media:credit: Adrianna Newell for The New York Times
item.media:description: New York legislators passed a one-week extension to the April 1 deadline for approval of the state budget.

item.title: Will the Iran Crisis Push the World to Rethink Oil and Gas?
item.description: A growing energy crisis could push countries to burn more fossil fuels in the short term, experts say. Plus more climate news.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Carolyn Van Houten for The New York Times
item.media:description: Demonstrators chanted on Tuesday outside the Interior Department, where the God Squad meeting was held.

item.title: ‘God Squad’ Waives Environmental Rules for Drilling in the Gulf of Mexico
item.description: The panel voted to override Endangered Species Act restrictions on oil and gas activities in the Gulf of Mexico, home to critically endangered whales and other imperiled wildlife.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Carolyn Van Houten for The New York Times
item.media:description: Demonstrators outside the Interior Department in Washington on Tuesday.

item.title: Edward Russo Says Donald Trump Is an ‘Environmental Hero’
item.description: Edward Russo, the only member of a White House task force, thinks the president doesn’t get enough credit for conservation at his golf courses, among other things.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times

item.title: Vermont Hits Back at Trump’s Effort to Block ‘Climate Superfund’ Law
item.description: The law would make fuel companies help pay for damages caused by climate change. The administration argues it’s unconstitutional.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Caleb Kenna for The New York Times
item.media:description: The federal courthouse in Rutland, Vt., on Monday, where the Justice Department was suing to block the state’s superfund law.

item.title: Why the Iran War May Force Countries to Rely Less on Natural Gas
item.description: The U.S. and other exporters are poised for a windfall, but disruptions to Persian Gulf supplies are also pushing gas-buying countries to consider alternatives like coal, solar and nuclear energy.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Brad Plumer
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Brandon Bell/Getty Images
item.media:description: A liquefied natural gas plant in Cameron, La., last year. The United States, the world’s biggest gas exporter, will almost certainly benefit from shortages caused by the war in Iran.

item.title: The Fragile Hope for Salmon Recovery in Maine
item.description: A long-term project to remove or modify dams may clear the way for endangered wild Atlantic salmon to swim freely up to the Sandy River. But it faces opposition from business and lawmakers.
item.dc:creator: Murray Carpenter and Greta Rybus
item.media:credit: Greta Rybus for The New York Times

item.title: Clean Energy Companies Are Trying to Survive the Trump Era
item.description: Offshore wind is out. Geothermal power is in. And many climate technology start-ups are looking for ways to carry on without federal backing.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Danielle Villasana/Reuters
item.media:description: A tour of the Geothermal House of the CERAWeek energy conference in Houston this week. Geothermal energy is emerging as one of the big green winners of the current era.

item.title: He Helped Write the Clean Air Act. He Fears for Its Future.
item.description: Thomas Jorling, adviser to Republicans who cosponsored the 1970 law, disputes the Trump administration’s claim that it shouldn’t apply to planet-warming greenhouse gases.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Cindy Schultz for The New York Times

item.title: Japan and the U.S. Agree to Team Up on Seabed Mining
item.description: The arrangement could signal a fracture in the decades-long effort among nations to reach consensus on how to mine the ocean floor while protecting ecosystems.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:credit: Yuka Obayashi/Reuters
item.media:description: A Japanese seabed mining research vessel in Shimizu, Japan, in January.

item.title: The Iran War Is Revealing the Messy Middle of Our Renewable Energy Transition
item.description: When the world map of literal power changes, the political hierarchy shifts, too.
item.dc:creator: David Wallace-Wells
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: What the Energy Industry Is (and Isn’t) Saying About the War in Iran
item.description: The latest from a closely watched gathering of energy industry executives, a rare look at a whale birth and more news.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Danielle Villasana/Reuters
item.media:description: Attendees of the CERAWeek energy conference in Houston on Monday.

item.title: Winter Sea Ice in the Arctic Ties a Record Low
item.description: Ice plays a vital role in reflecting away planet-warming sunlight. The Arctic is warming much faster than most other parts of the world.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Harry Stevens
item.category: National Snow and Ice Data Center
item.media:credit: Olivier Morin/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The disappearing ice also affects wildlife habitat. A polar bear on Norwegian sea ice last April.

item.title: Scientists Filmed a Whale Birth. The Surprise: Mom Had Many Helpers.
item.description: The episode, involving a group of sperm whales, adds to evidence that humans aren’t the only species that gets some form of assistance during and after delivery.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn

item.title: Wealthy Investors Target Foes of Clean Energy, Seeking Revenge
item.description: Renewable energy leaders said their industry got “rolled” in President Trump’s tax bill. Now they’re fighting back, starting in Texas.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman and Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Mason Trinca for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Roseland solar and storage facility in Riesel, Texas. The state ranks first in wind and second in solar capacity in the country, generating nearly $300 million in state and local taxes annually.
